skill.name: gepetto
skill.description: Guides Claude Code through research, interviews, plan synthesis, external review, and sectioned implementation planning for complex software work.
skill.summary: Creates reviewed, sectioned implementation plans from rough software specs.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are markdown fences, file names, examples, or .md references rather than executable package code. The confirmed risk is workflow-driven: the skill instructs Claude Code to run external review CLIs and optional autonomous execution tools on user planning content, so publication should include a clear external-tool and data-sharing warning.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Review Sends Plans to Third-Party Tools
medium_findings.description: The skill directs Claude Code to invoke Gemini and Codex CLIs with the generated implementation plan. This is legitimate review functionality, but it can disclose proprietary requirements or codebase context to external providers and depends on locally configured CLIs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly launch Gemini and Codex review commands and pass the plan content into them. The behavior is intentional, but it is a real data-sharing and external-command risk.

medium_findings.title: Non-Interactive External Review Modes Increase Blast Radius
medium_findings.description: The external review protocol uses Gemini with approval-mode yolo and Codex with full-auto. If the reviewed plan contains hostile prompt text, the external reviewer may run with fewer interaction checkpoints than a cautious manual review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and notes show non-interactive flags. I did not find bundled malware, but the reduced approval friction is a meaningful operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Optional Autonomous Execution Workflows Can Modify Repositories
medium_findings.description: The skill creates prompts and PRD files for ralph-loop and Ralphy, including options for parallel execution, branch-per-task, and pull request creation. These are documented optional workflows, but users should review generated plans before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show generation of autonomous execution artifacts and examples for external CLI execution. The risk is downstream misuse or overbroad automation rather than malicious package code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Triggered Command Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external-command hits are fenced markdown examples, literal tool names, file names, or usage snippets. They are not hidden scripts and do not execute during installation or loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct inspection shows static hits in documentation blocks and templates. The package contains markdown instructions only, with no separate executable source files.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Skill Installation Example
low_findings.description: The README shows a manual copy command into ~/.claude/skills. This is normal for Claude Code skill installation, but it does write into a user configuration directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden-directory findings map to installation documentation and a file tree example. I found no evidence of covert hidden-file access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are .md Reference False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to be triggered by markdown file extensions and documentation text rather than cryptographic code. No hashing or encryption implementation was found in the inspected skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are metadata, question templates, or markdown file path examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution of Plan Content into Review CLIs
dangerous_patterns.description: The external review examples interpolate claude-plan.md into Gemini and Codex CLI prompts using shell command substitution. This can expose plan content to external tools and should only run after user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippets are explicit and semantically tied to the review workflow. They are documented examples, but the pattern is still sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Research Uses WebSearch and WebFetch
dangerous_patterns.description: The research protocol can send derived topics to web search and fetch tools. This is expected for current best-practice research, but users should avoid sensitive project names or secrets in research queries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly instruct web research with source fetching. The behavior is optional and user-selected, so the risk is moderate.

content.user_title: Plan Complex Features with Multi-LLM Review
content.value_statement: Complex features fail when requirements, risks, and dependencies are unclear. Gepetto turns a rough markdown spec into a researched, reviewed, sectioned implementation plan.
actual_capabilities: Validates that the user provided a markdown spec file before starting.
Guides codebase and web research decisions before writing a plan.
Runs a structured stakeholder interview to clarify requirements and edge cases.
Synthesizes research and interview answers into specification and plan documents.
Requests independent Gemini and Codex reviews when configured by the user.
Splits the final plan into self-contained implementation section files.
limitations: Requires Claude Code style file and tool access to work as designed.
External reviews require locally installed and configured Gemini or Codex CLIs.
Generated plans still need human review before autonomous implementation.
Web research can expose search topics to external services.

use_cases.title: Clarify a Vague Feature Request
use_cases.description: Convert a short feature idea into requirements, research notes, decisions, and implementation sections before coding starts.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Architecture Review
use_cases.description: Create a self-contained plan and collect independent review feedback from configured LLM CLIs.

use_cases.title: Hand Off Parallel Implementation Work
use_cases.description: Split a large plan into section files with dependencies, acceptance criteria, and implementation context.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with a short markdown feature spec.

prompt_templates.title: Plan With Codebase Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Use existing repository patterns before drafting the implementation plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gepetto with this spec. Research the current codebase patterns before writing the final plan.

prompt_templates.title: Plan With Current Best Practices
prompt_templates.scenario: Add web research for technologies or integrations mentioned in the spec.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gepetto for this spec. Include web research for the authentication, session storage, and deployment topics.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Autonomous Execution Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Create reviewed sections and downstream execution artifacts for a large implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full gepetto workflow, include external review, then generate section files and execution documents after I approve the plan.

output_examples.input: A short request for OAuth login, Redis sessions, and API tokens.
output_examples.output: A researched specification, interview transcript, reviewed implementation plan, and ordered section files for authentication, storage, middleware, and tests.

output_examples.input: A migration idea with unclear dependency order and several unknown services.
output_examples.output: A plan that documents assumptions, open questions, integration risks, dependency order, and self-contained work sections for implementation.

output_examples.input: A large refactor that needs independent review before work begins.
output_examples.output: A review-ready plan with Gemini and Codex feedback notes, integration decisions, and revised implementation sections.

best_practices: Start with a markdown spec that names the goal, users, constraints, and known risks.
Review generated plans before running ralph-loop, Ralphy, or any external execution tool.
Avoid putting secrets, customer data, or private identifiers in prompts used for web research or external review.
anti_patterns: Using Gepetto for small bug fixes that do not need research or planning.
Skipping the interview and treating the first generated plan as final.
Running autonomous implementation commands before checking dependencies and acceptance criteria.

faq.question: Does Gepetto write application code?
faq.answer: It focuses on planning. It creates specifications, plans, review notes, and section files for later implementation.

faq.question: Does it require external CLI tools?
faq.answer: The core planning flow can run without them. Gemini, Codex, ralph-loop, and Ralphy are optional integrations.

faq.question: Can it resume an interrupted planning session?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks existing planning files and resumes from the matching workflow step.

faq.question: What files does it create?
faq.answer: It creates markdown planning files such as research notes, interview notes, a plan, review files, and section files.

faq.question: Is it safe for private projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. External review and web research can share plan content or search topics with outside services.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for engineers and leads planning complex features, migrations, or multi-step implementation work.
